_comment: Generic UEFI EFI variable boot order manipulation. Works on any UEFI system (Lenovo, Dell, HP, etc.) as long as the process has SeSystemEnvironmentPrivilege. Used as fallback when vendor-specific tools aren't available.

BootOrder.name: BootOrder
BootOrder.description: Ordered list of BootXXXX entry numbers. Index 0 = first boot device.

BootCurrent.name: BootCurrent
BootCurrent.description: Boot entry number that was used for the current boot.

BootNext.name: BootNext
BootNext.description: One-time boot override — UEFI uses this entry for the NEXT boot only, then deletes it. Write a Boot entry number here for one-shot boot.

BootXXXX.description: Individual boot entries (Boot0000, Boot0001, etc.). Each contains: Attributes(uint32) + FilePath(EFI_DEVICE_PATH) + Description(UTF-16). Read Description field to identify PXE, Windows Boot Manager, etc.

uefi_attributes.NV_BS_RT: 0x7 (NonVolatile | BootService | RuntimeService) — standard for boot variables

change_boot_order: 2. Enumerate Boot0000..Boot9999 to find entries matching the target (e.g., PXE = description contains 'PXE' or 'Network')
3. Move target entry number(s) to front of BootOrder array
4. Write new BootOrder back with SetFirmwareEnvironmentVariableEx
Note: changes take effect on next boot
one_shot_boot: 1. Enumerate Boot entries to find the target
2. Write that entry's number to BootNext variable
3. UEFI boots that entry once and then removes BootNext automatically

caveats: Requires SeSystemEnvironmentPrivilege — must run elevated with UAC or as SYSTEM.
Entry description strings vary by firmware vendor and NIC model — use pattern matching.
Some firmware vendors (e.g., HP) use non-standard EFI variable layouts.
Writing BootOrder directly bypasses vendor BIOS logic — may behave unexpectedly on some hardware.
implementation_note: See Set-BiosBootTarget.ps1 for the PowerShell implementation using Invoke-UefiBootOrder.ps1
